title: Credit scoring — what it measures and what it misses
subtitle: A credit score is a prediction of repayment behavior based on historical data. It is useful, limited, and frequently misunderstood.

body.content: A credit score is a three-digit number — in most markets, between 300 and 850 — that represents a statistical estimate of the probability that you will repay your debts on time. Lenders use it to determine whether to extend credit and at what interest rate. A higher score means lower predicted risk, which translates to better borrowing terms.

body.label: The five factors in a FICO score
body.content: Payment history (35%): whether you have paid past credit obligations on time. Amounts owed (30%): how much of your available credit you are using — utilization rate. Length of credit history (15%): how long your accounts have been open. New credit (10%): recent applications for new credit. Credit mix (10%): the variety of credit types you hold.

Credit scores are backward-looking — they measure past behavior, not current financial health. A high earner who recently paid off significant debt may score lower than a moderate earner with a longer, cleaner history. Scores also miss wealth, income stability, and assets entirely. Two people with identical scores may have dramatically different financial positions.

The practical strategy for optimizing your score: pay on time without exception, keep utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%), avoid unnecessary new credit applications, and maintain older accounts even if unused. These behaviors signal low risk to statistical models — which is the outcome the score was designed to measure.

A credit score is a useful tool for accessing debt on favorable terms. Treat it as a means, not an end. The goal is not a high score — the goal is a financial life that produces a high score as a natural byproduct.
